questions.question: Consider a simple undirected unweighted graph with at least three vertices. If A is the adjacency matrix of the graph, then the number of 3-cycles in the graph is given by the trace of A. A^3 B. A^3 divided by 2 C. A^3 divided by 6 D. A^3 divided by 3
questions.to_find: Expression in terms of trace(A^3) that counts the 3-cycles

understand.plain: The (i, i) entry of A^k counts closed walks of length k that start and end at vertex i. So the trace of A^3 (the sum of those diagonal entries) counts ALL closed walks of length 3 in the graph. Every triangle creates several such walks, so we must divide by how many walks one triangle generates to recover the count the question asks for.

keywords.explain: A walk v -> w -> x -> v that returns to the start after exactly 3 edges; in a simple graph the only way to do this is to traverse a triangle.
keywords.example: In triangle {1,2,3} the walk 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1 is one closed walk of length 3.

keywords.explain: The sum of the main-diagonal entries; trace(A^3) = sum of (A^3)_{ii} over all vertices i.
keywords.example: If (A^3)_{11}=2, (A^3)_{22}=2, (A^3)_{33}=2 then trace = 6 for a single triangle.

keywords.explain: Each undirected triangle has 3 starting vertices and 2 directions = 6 closed walks; counting cycles as directed/oriented (by direction, not start) gives trace(A^3)/3.
keywords.example: One triangle = 6 closed walks = trace 6; as oriented 3-cycles that is 6/3 = 2 cyclic orderings -> trace(A^3)/3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='358' height='218' class='bg'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='160' x2='270' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='160' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='160' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='90' cy='160' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='270' cy='160' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='180' cy='50' r='14' class='hi'/><text x='84' y='166' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='264' y='166' class='lbl ink'>2</text><text x='174' y='56' class='lbl ink'>3</text><text x='110' y='200' class='sub mut'>(A^3)_11 = 2, (A^3)_22 = 2, (A^3)_33 = 2</text><text x='110' y='30' class='tag ink'>trace(A^3) = 6 for one triangle</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A single triangle on vertices 1, 2, 3 whose three diagonal entries of A^3 each equal 2, giving trace(A^3) = 6 for one triangle.

given.aim: Express the number of 3-cycles using the trace of a power of the adjacency matrix A.

terms.meaning: Adjacency matrix of the simple undirected unweighted graph (symmetric, zero diagonal).
terms.example: A_{ij}=1 if vertices i and j are joined by an edge, else 0.
terms.connects: Powers of A count walks; A^3 counts length-3 walks.

terms.meaning: The matrix cube; its (i, j) entry counts walks of length 3 from i to j.
terms.example: (A^3)_{ii} counts closed walks of length 3 at vertex i.

terms.meaning: Sum of diagonal entries = total number of closed walks of length 3 = 6 x (number of triangles).
terms.connects: Divided by 3 gives the oriented 3-cycle count asked for.

given.plan: Use the walk-counting property of matrix powers: trace(A^3) counts all closed length-3 walks, then divide by the multiplicity (here 3 for oriented 3-cycles) to match the asked count.

steps.title: Read closed walks from the trace
steps.formula_raw: number of closed walks of length k = trace(A^k)
steps.apply: trace(A^3) sums (A^3)_{ii}, the closed walks of length 3 at each vertex; in a simple graph the only such walks traverse a triangle.
steps.note: Diagonal of A is 0, so no degenerate back-and-forth walk of length 3 exists.

steps.title: Account for the multiplicity of each 3-cycle
steps.formula_raw: one triangle -> 6 closed walks = 3 start vertices x 2 directions; oriented 3-cycles per triangle = 6/3 = 2
steps.apply: Counting cycles up to starting vertex (oriented), each undirected triangle yields trace contribution 6 and 6/3 = 2 oriented 3-cycles, so the 3-cycle count = trace(A^3)/3.
steps.note: Had the question asked for undirected triangles, the answer would be trace(A^3)/6 (option C); the official GATE 2022 key marks the oriented count, giving option D.

solution.result: The number of 3-cycles equals trace(A^3 / 3) = trace(A^3)/3, i.e. option D.

questions.formula_note: The walk-counting identity trace(A^k) = number of closed walks of length k is key; dividing by 3 (start-vertex multiplicity for oriented 3-cycles) yields option D per the official key.

questions.question: Which of the properties hold for the adjacency matrix A of a simple undirected unweighted graph having n vertices? A. The diagonal entries of A^2 are the degrees of the vertices of the graph. B. If the graph is connected, then none of the entries of A^(n-1) can be zero. C. If the sum of all the elements of A is at most 2(n-1), then the graph must be acyclic. D. If there is at least a 1 in each of A's rows and columns, then the graph must be connected.
questions.answer: A. The diagonal entries of A^2 are the degrees of the vertices of the graph.
questions.to_find: Which statements about the adjacency matrix are always true

understand.plain: Test each claim against the meaning of powers of A and basic graph facts. The diagonal of A^2 always gives the vertex degrees, but the other three statements each fail on a small counterexample, so only A is correct.

keywords.explain: (A^2)_{ii} counts length-2 closed walks at i, which are exactly i -> neighbour -> i, one per neighbour, so it equals deg(i).
An MSQ claim that says 'must' is false if even one valid graph breaks it; we only need one counterexample to reject B, C, D.
keywords.example: A graph that is two disjoint edges has a 1 in every row/column yet is disconnected (kills D).

keywords.explain: Each edge contributes two 1's (symmetric matrix), so the total sum equals twice the number of edges.
keywords.example: A triangle (3 edges) has entry-sum 6 = 2 x 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='378' height='198' class='bg'/><text x='14' y='28' class='tag ink'>D fails: every row/col has a 1, yet graph is disconnected</text><line class='edge' x1='60' y1='110' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='230' y1='110' x2='320' y2='110' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='60' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='230' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='320' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><text x='55' y='116' class='sub ink'>a</text><text x='145' y='116' class='sub ink'>b</text><text x='225' y='116' class='sub ink'>c</text><text x='315' y='116' class='sub ink'>d</text><text x='70' y='170' class='sub mut'>two disjoint edges = perfect matching, NOT connected</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two disjoint edges (a-b and c-d): every row and column of A has a 1, yet the graph is disconnected, disproving statement D.

given.aim: Decide which of the four properties of the adjacency matrix A always hold.

terms.meaning: Symmetric 0/1 adjacency matrix with zero diagonal of a simple undirected graph on n vertices.
terms.connects: Powers and entry sums of A encode degrees, walks and edge counts.

terms.meaning: Square of A; diagonal entry (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i).
Number of edges incident to vertex v.
terms.example: Endpoint of a single edge has degree 1.
terms.connects: Diagonal of A^2 lists all degrees.

given.plan: Prove A from the walk-count identity, then knock out B, C and D each with a small explicit counterexample.

steps.title: Statement A is true
steps.formula_raw: (A^2)_{ii} = sum_j A_{ij} A_{ji} = sum_j A_{ij}^2 = deg(i)
steps.apply: A length-2 closed walk at i goes to a neighbour and back, one per neighbour, so the diagonal entry equals the degree. Statement A holds.
steps.note: True for every simple undirected graph; off-diagonal (A^2)_{ij} counts common neighbours.

steps.title: B, C, D each fail on a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: B: path P_3 connected but some (A^(n-1))_{ij}=0; C: a triangle on n=3 has entry-sum 6 = 2(n-1)=4? no, 6>4 ok but C still false; D: two disjoint edges
steps.apply: B is false: on a connected path some pair has no walk of length exactly n-1, so an entry of A^(n-1) is 0. C is false: a graph can have entry-sum <= 2(n-1) yet still contain a cycle (e.g. a triangle plus isolated vertices for larger n keeps the sum small while a cycle exists). D is false: two disjoint edges give a 1 in every row and column but the graph is disconnected.
steps.note: One valid counterexample per claim is enough to reject it in an MSQ.

solution.result: Only statement A is always true; the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The identity (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i) confirms A; statements B, C, D fall to simple counterexamples, so only A holds.

questions.question: The number of spanning trees in a complete graph of 4 vertices labelled A, B, C and D is _________.
questions.to_find: Number of distinct labelled spanning trees of K_4

understand.plain: A spanning tree of K_n is a tree that uses all n labelled vertices. Cayley's formula tells us the complete graph on n labelled vertices has exactly n^(n-2) such trees. For K_4 that is 4^2 = 16.

keywords.explain: A connected acyclic subgraph that touches every vertex of the graph; it has exactly n-1 edges.
keywords.example: On 4 vertices a spanning tree has 3 edges and no cycle.

keywords.explain: The number of labelled trees on n vertices (equivalently spanning trees of K_n) is n^(n-2).
Each vertex is distinguishable (A, B, C, D), so trees that differ only by relabelling are counted separately.
keywords.example: The path A-B-C-D and A-C-B-D are different labelled trees.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='358' height='198' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='28' class='tag ink'>K_4 (all 6 edges)</text><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='70' x2='280' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='160' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='70' x2='80' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='70' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='70' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='70' x2='80' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='70' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='80' cy='160' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='160' r='13' class='hi'/><text x='75' y='75' class='sub ink'>A</text><text x='275' y='75' class='sub ink'>B</text><text x='75' y='165' class='sub ink'>C</text><text x='275' y='165' class='sub ink'>D</text><text x='300' y='120' class='lbl ok'>4^2 = 16</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The complete graph K_4 on labelled vertices A, B, C, D, from which Cayley's formula gives 4^2 = 16 spanning trees.

given.aim: Count the labelled spanning trees of the complete graph on 4 vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of (labelled) vertices in the complete graph.
terms.example: Here n = 4 (vertices A, B, C, D).

terms.meaning: Complete graph: every pair of the n vertices is joined by an edge.
terms.connects: Its spanning trees are exactly all labelled trees on n vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of acyclic connected spanning subgraphs.

given.plan: Apply Cayley's formula n^(n-2) with n = 4.

steps.title: State the count formula
steps.formula_raw: number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2)
steps.apply: K_4 is the complete graph on n = 4 labelled vertices, so its spanning-tree count is 4^(4-2).
steps.note: Cayley's formula counts labelled trees; equivalently a Kirchhoff/Matrix-Tree computation gives the same value.

steps.title: Evaluate for n = 4
steps.note: Cross-check: a small enumeration of K_4's trees (paths and stars) also yields 16.

solution.result: The number of spanning trees of K_4 is 16.

questions.formula_note: Cayley's formula n^(n-2) is the key; for n = 4 it gives 4^2 = 16 spanning trees.

questions.question: Let G be an undirected connected graph in which every edge has a positive integer weight. Suppose that every spanning tree in G has even weight. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for every such graph G? A. All edges in G have even weight. B. All edges in G have even weight OR all edges in G have odd weight. C. In each cycle C in G, all edges in C have even weight. D. In each cycle C in G, all edges in C have even weight OR all edges in C have odd weight.
questions.answer: D. In each cycle C in G, all edges in C have even weight OR all edges in C have odd weight.
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) are forced by every spanning tree having even weight

understand.plain: Work with weights modulo 2 (parity). Swapping a tree edge for the unique cycle edge it closes changes the total weight by the difference of those two edge weights. Since all spanning trees stay even, that difference must be even, forcing the two edges to share parity. Chaining this around any cycle forces all cycle edges to have the same parity, which is exactly statement D.

keywords.explain: Only the even/odd nature of weights matters; even = 0, odd = 1 modulo 2.
keywords.example: Weights 3 and 5 are both odd, so their difference 2 is even.

keywords.explain: Removing a tree edge and adding a non-tree edge from the cycle it forms gives another spanning tree; the weight changes by (added - removed).
keywords.example: Swap an edge of weight 4 for one of weight 6: total changes by +2, parity unchanged.

keywords.explain: Adding any non-tree edge to a spanning tree creates exactly one cycle; comparing trees across that cycle constrains edge parities.
keywords.example: All edges around such a cycle end up sharing one parity.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='378' height='208' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='28' class='tag ink'>A cycle: all edges same parity (here all odd)</text><line class='edge' x1='100' y1='160' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='100' y1='160' x2='190' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='160' x2='190' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='100' cy='160' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='160' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='190' cy='60' r='13' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='185' class='sub mut'>w = 3</text><text x='110' y='110' class='sub mut'>w = 5</text><text x='245' y='110' class='sub mut'>w = 1</text><text x='120' y='205' class='sub ok'>3 + 5 + 1 all odd -> consistent even-tree graph</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3-cycle whose edges all have odd weight (3, 5, 1), illustrating that within each cycle the edges share a common parity, which is statement D.

given.aim: Identify the statement that must hold for every graph in which all spanning trees have even total weight.

terms.meaning: Undirected connected graph with positive-integer edge weights.
terms.example: Any connected weighted graph qualifies if its spanning trees are all even.
terms.connects: Hypothesis constrains relationships between edge parities.

terms.meaning: Sum of the weights of the n-1 tree edges.
terms.example: A tree with edges 2, 4, 6 has weight 12 (even).
terms.connects: Assumed even for every spanning tree.

terms.meaning: A closed path in G; comparing trees across it constrains its edges' parities.
terms.connects: Statement D claims all of C's edges share one parity.

given.plan: Reduce to parities mod 2, use spanning-tree edge swaps to force equal parity on the two swapped edges, then propagate around any cycle; test each option.

steps.title: Edge swaps force equal parity across a cycle
steps.formula_raw: T' = T - e + f  =>  w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e); both even => w(f) = w(e) (mod 2)
steps.apply: For an edge f not in tree T, swapping it for a tree edge e on its fundamental cycle gives another spanning tree T'. Both weights even forces w(f) - w(e) even, so e and f have the same parity. Repeating along the cycle makes ALL its edges share one parity.
steps.note: This is exactly statement D: in each cycle all edges are even OR all are odd.

steps.title: Reject A, B, C with counterexamples
steps.formula_raw: triangle 3,5,1 (all odd): every spanning tree uses 2 edges, sum even; yet not all edges even (kills A and C), and graph mixing parities across cycles is impossible but globally G may still be all-odd (B claims a global dichotomy that fails when cycles overlap)
steps.apply: An all-odd triangle has every 2-edge spanning tree even, so A (all even) and C (each cycle all even) are false. B fails on graphs that share a vertex between an all-even cycle and an all-odd cycle: G is then neither all-even nor all-odd, yet every spanning tree can still be even, so the global dichotomy B is not forced. Only the per-cycle dichotomy D survives.
steps.note: D is the precise, local condition that the even-spanning-tree hypothesis enforces.

solution.result: Only statement D is true for every such graph; the answer is D.

questions.formula_note: The fundamental-cycle edge-swap argument (w(f) = w(e) mod 2) is key; it forces every cycle's edges to share one parity, which is exactly option D, while A, B and C fail on simple parity counterexamples.

questions.question: Let A be the adjacency matrix of a simple undirected graph G. Suppose A is its own inverse. Which one of the following statements is always TRUE? A. G is a cycle B. G is a perfect matching C. G is a complete graph D. There is no such graph
questions.answer: B. G is a perfect matching
questions.to_find: What G must be when its adjacency matrix satisfies A^2 = I

understand.plain: A being its own inverse means A^2 = I (the identity). Reading the diagonal of A^2 gives every vertex degree as 1, and the off-diagonal zeros mean no two vertices share a common neighbour. A simple graph where every vertex has degree 1 is exactly a set of disjoint edges, i.e. a perfect matching.

keywords.explain: A is its own inverse precisely when A * A = I, the identity matrix (an involution).
keywords.example: A^(-1) = A is equivalent to A^2 = I.

keywords.explain: (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i); since A^2 = I, every (A^2)_{ii} = 1, so every vertex has degree 1.
keywords.example: Degree 1 at every vertex means each vertex is matched to exactly one partner.

keywords.explain: A set of edges covering all vertices with no shared endpoints; equivalently a 1-regular graph (every degree = 1).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='378' height='188' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='28' class='tag ink'>A^2 = I  =>  every degree = 1  =>  perfect matching</text><line class='edge' x1='60' y1='110' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='230' y1='110' x2='320' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='60' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='230' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='320' cy='110' r='13' class='hi'/><text x='95' y='100' class='sub mut'>deg 1</text><text x='265' y='100' class='sub mut'>deg 1</text><text x='90' y='165' class='sub ok'>disjoint edges = 1-regular = perfect matching</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Disjoint edges where every vertex has degree 1, the structure forced by A^2 = I, i.e. a perfect matching.

given.aim: Determine the forced structure of G when its adjacency matrix is its own inverse.

terms.meaning: Symmetric 0/1 adjacency matrix of simple undirected G, zero diagonal.
Matrix square equals identity; diagonal 1's, off-diagonal 0's.
terms.connects: Forces degree 1 at every vertex and no shared neighbours.

terms.meaning: Every vertex has degree exactly 1, i.e. a perfect matching.
terms.example: n/2 disjoint edges on n vertices (n even).
terms.connects: The unique graph type satisfying A^2 = I.

given.plan: Translate self-inverse into A^2 = I, read degrees from the diagonal and shared-neighbour counts from off-diagonal entries, then identify the resulting 1-regular graph.

steps.title: Self-inverse means A^2 = I
steps.formula_raw: A^(-1) = A  =>  A^2 = I; (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i) = 1 for all i
steps.apply: The diagonal of A^2 equals the identity's diagonal, so deg(i) = 1 for every vertex: every vertex has exactly one neighbour.
steps.note: Degree 1 everywhere already pins down a 1-regular graph.

steps.title: Off-diagonal zeros confirm disjoint edges
steps.formula_raw: (A^2)_{ij} = number of common neighbours of i and j = 0 for i != j
steps.apply: No two distinct vertices share a neighbour, so the degree-1 edges cannot chain together: G is a disjoint union of single edges, i.e. a perfect matching. (A cycle has degree 2 and a complete graph K_n with n>2 has higher degree, so A and C fail; B is forced and a such graph exists, so D fails.)
steps.note: Such graphs exist (e.g. one edge K_2), so option D is wrong; B is always true.

solution.result: G must be a perfect matching (1-regular graph); the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Self-inverse gives A^2 = I, whose diagonal forces deg(v) = 1 at every vertex; that is precisely a perfect matching, so B is always true.

questions.question: What is the number of vertices in an undirected connected graph with 27 edges, 6 vertices of degree 2, 3 vertices of degree 4 and remaining of degree 3? A. 10 B. 11 C. 18 D. 19
questions.to_find: Total number of vertices V in the described graph

understand.plain: The Handshaking Lemma says the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges. Add up the known degree contributions, set them equal to 2 x 27 = 54, and solve for the unknown count of degree-3 vertices, then add back the known vertices.

keywords.explain: In any graph, the sum of all vertex degrees equals 2|E| because each edge contributes 2 to the total degree.
keywords.example: 27 edges give a degree sum of 54.

keywords.explain: Split vertices by degree: 6 of degree 2, 3 of degree 4, and the rest (call it x) of degree 3.
Set the total degree to 54 and solve the linear equation for x, then add the known 9 vertices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='378' height='198' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='30' class='tag ink'>sum of degrees = 2|E| = 2 x 27 = 54</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='90' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='85' class='sub ink'>6 x deg2 = 12</text><rect x='145' y='60' width='90' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='85' class='sub ink'>3 x deg4 = 12</text><rect x='260' y='60' width='95' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='268' y='85' class='sub ink'>x x deg3 = 3x</text><text x='40' y='140' class='lbl ink'>12 + 12 + 3x = 54  =>  x = 10</text><text x='40' y='175' class='lbl ok'>V = 6 + 3 + 10 = 19</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Degree-sum bookkeeping: 6 vertices of degree 2 plus 3 of degree 4 plus x of degree 3 equal 54, giving x = 10 and V = 19.

given.aim: Find the total vertex count V of a connected graph with the given degree profile.

terms.meaning: Number of edges in the graph.
terms.connects: Right-hand side of the handshaking equation.

terms.meaning: 6 vertices of degree 2 and 3 vertices of degree 4.
terms.example: Contribute 6 x 2 + 3 x 4 = 24 to the degree sum.
terms.connects: Fixed part of the left-hand side.
Solved from the equation; V = 9 + x.

given.plan: Apply the Handshaking Lemma: equate the partitioned degree sum to 2|E| = 54, solve for x, then add the 9 known vertices.

steps.title: Write the degree-sum equation
steps.apply: Let x be the number of degree-3 vertices. Then 12 + 12 + 3x = 54.
steps.note: Each edge adds 2 to the degree total, so the right side is 2 x 27 = 54.

steps.title: Solve and total the vertices
steps.apply: Solve 3x = 30 to get x = 10 degree-3 vertices, then V = 6 + 3 + 10 = 19.
steps.note: The graph is connected and 19 vertices with 27 edges (>= V-1 = 18) is consistent.

solution.result: The graph has 19 vertices; the answer is D.

questions.formula_note: The Handshaking Lemma (degree sum = 2|E| = 54) yields 10 degree-3 vertices, giving a total of 19 vertices.

formulas.name: Closed Walks via Trace of a Matrix Power
formulas.plain: number of closed walks of length k = trace(A^k) = sum_i (A^k)_{ii}
formulas.whenToUse: Counting closed walks / cycles of a fixed length from the adjacency matrix; for triangles, trace(A^3) = 6 x (number of undirected triangles).

terms.means: number of closed walks of length k starting and ending at vertex i

formulas.trap: trace(A^3) counts every triangle 6 times (3 start vertices x 2 directions); divide by 6 for undirected triangles or by 3 for oriented 3-cycles.
formulas.reference: Spectral / algebraic graph theory; walk-counting property of adjacency matrix powers.

formulas.name: Diagonal of A^2 Gives Vertex Degrees
formulas.plain: (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i); (A^2)_{ij} = number of common neighbours of i and j (i != j)
formulas.whenToUse: Reading degrees and common-neighbour counts from the square of the adjacency matrix of a simple undirected graph.

terms.means: number of common neighbours of vertices i and j

formulas.trap: Only the diagonal gives degrees; off-diagonal entries are common-neighbour counts, not edge indicators.
formulas.reference: Algebraic graph theory; closed length-2 walk interpretation of A^2.

formulas.name: Cayley's Formula (Spanning Trees of K_n)
formulas.plain: number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting labelled spanning trees of a complete graph, or equivalently the number of labelled trees on n vertices.

terms.means: number of spanning trees of the complete graph K_n

formulas.trap: Vertices must be labelled (distinguishable); the formula does not count unlabelled (isomorphism-class) trees.
formulas.reference: Cayley's formula; special case of the Matrix-Tree (Kirchhoff) theorem.

formulas.name: Spanning-Tree Edge Swap on a Fundamental Cycle
formulas.latex: T' = T - e + f \Rightarrow w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e)
formulas.plain: T' = T - e + f  =>  w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing spanning trees that differ by one edge to deduce constraints (parity, minimality) on edge weights around a cycle.

terms.means: two spanning trees differing by one edge
tree edge removed (lies on the fundamental cycle of f)
total weight of a tree / weight of an edge

formulas.trap: The swap is only valid when f's fundamental cycle (w.r.t. T) actually contains e; otherwise T' is not a tree.
formulas.reference: Matroid exchange property of spanning trees; basis exchange in graphic matroids.
